stem: Consider the data set \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]] \} \). What is the mean (arithmetic average) of these five values?
solution: The mean is the sum of the values divided by how many there are: \( \frac{[[x1]] + [[x2]] + [[x3]] + [[x4]] + [[x5]]}{5} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the total of the values; the mean also requires dividing by the count, 5.
This divides by 4 instead of 5; there are five values, not four.
This divides by 6 instead of 5; there are five values, not six.
This is off by one from the correct mean.
This is off by one from the correct mean.

stem: Consider the data set \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]] \} \), listed here in increasing order. What is the median of this data set?
solution: With five values already in increasing order, the median is the middle (3rd) value: \( [[x3]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the 2nd value, one position too low to be the middle value of five.
This is the 4th value, one position too high to be the middle value of five.
This is the midrange (average of the smallest and largest value), not the median.
This is the mean of the data set, not the median.
This is off by one from the correct median.

stem: Consider the data set \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]],\, [[x6]] \} \), listed here in increasing order. What is the median of this data set?
solution: With an even number of values (six), the median is the average of the two middle (3rd and 4th) values: \( \frac{[[x3]] + [[x4]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the 3rd value, forgetting to average it with the 4th value.
This uses only the 4th value, forgetting to average it with the 3rd value.
This averages the 2nd and 5th values instead of the two true middle values.
This is the mean of the data set, not the median.
This is off by one from the correct median.

stem: In the data set \( \{ [[v]],\, [[v]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]] \} \), what is the mode (the value that occurs most often)?
solution: The value \( [[v]] \) appears twice, while every other value appears only once, so \( [[v]] \) is the mode.
distractor_rationales: This value appears only once in the data set; it is not the most frequent value.
This value appears only once in the data set; it is not the most frequent value.
This value appears only once in the data set; it is not the most frequent value.
This is the mean of the data set, not the mode.
This is off by one from the correct mode.

stem: The data set \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, y \} \) has mean \( [[M]] \). What is the value of \( y \)?
solution: The five values must total \( 5\cdot[[M]] \), so \( y = 5\cdot[[M]] - ([[x1]] + [[x2]] + [[x3]] + [[x4]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the missing value simply equals the mean.
This multiplies the mean by 4 (the number of KNOWN values) instead of 5 (the total count).
This has the wrong sign, giving the negative of the correct missing value.
This divides the known sum by 4 before subtracting, instead of using the full known total.
This is off by one from the correct missing value.

stem: Consider the data set \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]] \} \), listed here in increasing order. By how much does the mean of this data set exceed the median? (A negative value means the mean is smaller than the median.)
solution: The mean is \( \frac{[[x1]]+[[x2]]+[[x3]]+[[x4]]+[[x5]]}{5} \) and the median is \( [[x3]] \); their difference is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign; it computes median minus mean instead of mean minus median.
This reports the mean itself, forgetting to subtract the median.
This reports the median itself, forgetting to subtract it from the mean.
This uses the midrange (average of smallest and largest value) in place of the mean.
This is off by one from the correct difference.
